correctness.rationale: Core attribution/view logic is sound, but lack of foreign keys and a predictions table key mismatch risk silent join failures or write-time conflicts.

robustness.rationale: ADR-0001 trigger machinery, sentinel views, and defensive LATERAL dedup are thoughtfully implemented, though trigger sprawl and missing FKs invite drift.

scalability.rationale: Volumes are modest and most joins are indexed; one hot-path expression index is missing but partitioning isn’t warranted yet.

idiomaticness.rationale: Generally idiomatic Postgres (GIN/HNSW/LATERAL/partial indexes), but RLS policy targets are inconsistent and FKs are underused.

response.summary:
Overall, the pds schema is well-shaped for a single-user analytics system: attribution is centralized in derive_onyx_dates(), informative canary views catch TZ/ETL issues, and the behavioral matrix uses LATERAL de-duplication patterns that match the data semantics. JSONB is used judiciously where schemas are open-ended (supplement ingredients, Spotify payloads), and the journal embeddings are indexed correctly for vector search.

The riskiest areas are referential integrity and a subtle modeling/DDL mismatch: only one foreign key exists across many fact/dim relationships (WHOOP, Spotify, Garmin laps), which makes silent orphaning possible and shifts error detection from writes to later joins. Separately, pds.hrv_predictions is defined with a composite PK that implies one row per (date, model, horizon), while the views and commentary assume multiple versions per triple; this discrepancy can lead to upsert conflicts or loss of run history. RLS is broadly consistent but several service policies target public instead of service_role, and some tables rely solely on service-role bypass rather than explicit service policies.

If I could only fix three things: (1) add NOT VALID foreign keys on core join paths (whoop_recovery/sleep→cycles; spotify_plays→tracks/artists; garmin_laps→activities via a unique on activities.activity_id) and validate after a quick orphan audit; (2) resolve the hrv_predictions key semantics by introducing a surrogate PK and making (date, model, horizon, model_version) unique so multiple runs can coexist; (3) normalize RLS by replacing any public-targeted service policies with service_role and adding explicit service_full_access policies on tables that currently rely on bypass. These are low-risk at current scale and buy strong write-time guarantees against future ETL drift.

findings.title: Add FKs: whoop_recovery/sleep.cycle_id → whoop_cycles.cycle_id
findings.description: Today there’s no FK enforcing that recovery/sleep rows reference an existing WHOOP cycle, so deletions or out-of-order loads can create orphans that join away silently in matrix views.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: 'Referential integrity — only 1 FK' lists whoop_recovery.cycle_id and whoop_sleep.cycle_id as notably absent.
findings.recommendation: Add NOT VALID FKs and then VALIDATE after an orphan check: ALTER TABLE pds.whoop_recovery ADD CONSTRAINT whoop_recovery_cycle_fkey FOREIGN KEY (cycle_id) REFERENCES pds.whoop_cycles(cycle_id) ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE RESTRICT NOT VALID; ALTER TABLE pds.whoop_sleep ADD CONSTRAINT whoop_sleep_cycle_fkey FOREIGN KEY (cycle_id) REFERENCES pds.whoop_cycles(cycle_id) ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE RESTRICT NOT VALID; then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT after backfill.

findings.title: Add FKs: spotify_plays.track_id/artist_id → dims
findings.description: Orphan plays are possible if a track/artist row is missing or removed; downstream joins (e.g., spotify_daily_signature) would undercount features subtly.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md 'Notably absent foreign keys' lists spotify_plays.track_id and artist_id FKs as absent.
findings.recommendation: Enforce with deferred, NOT VALID FKs to avoid ingest-order pain: ALTER TABLE pds.spotify_plays ADD CONSTRAINT spotify_plays_track_fkey FOREIGN KEY (track_id) REFERENCES pds.spotify_tracks(track_id) ON DELETE SET NULL DEFERRABLE INITIALLY DEFERRED NOT VALID; and similarly for artist_id→spotify_artists(artist_id); then VALIDATE after confirming no orphans.

findings.title: Garmin laps → activities FK (via unique activity_id on parent)
findings.description: Laps can become orphaned from activities; current parent PK is (activity_id, ts), so no simple FK exists.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: 'garmin_activity_laps.activity_id → garmin_activities.activity_id' listed among absent FKs; parent PK is composite.
findings.recommendation: If activity_id is globally unique in practice, add a parent-side unique and then the FK: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY IF NOT EXISTS uq_garmin_activities_activity_id ON pds.garmin_activities(activity_id); ALTER TABLE pds.garmin_activity_laps ADD CONSTRAINT laps_activity_fkey FOREIGN KEY (activity_id) REFERENCES pds.garmin_activities(activity_id) ON DELETE CASCADE NOT VALID; then VALIDATE. If not unique, revisit parent PK design.

findings.title: RLS service policies target public instead of service_role
findings.description: Several service_full_access policies grant ALL to public, broadening scope beyond service_role; while GRANTs still block anon writes, this is non-idiomatic and fragile.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: “Several (whoop_*, garmin_*, sync_log) target {public} instead. Likely early-development pattern.”
findings.recommendation: Normalize to service_role: for each affected table, DROP POLICY service_full_access (or rename) and CREATE POLICY service_full_access ON pds.<table> FOR ALL TO service_role USING (true) WITH CHECK (true); review that no policy grants ALL to public.

findings.title: Tables rely on service bypass with no explicit service policy
findings.file_ref: pds.meal_events, pds.journal_entries, pds.myfitnesspal_nutrition, pds.spotify_*, pds.supplement_*, pds.weight_log, pds.habit_metadata_history
findings.description: A handful of tables have RLS enabled with anon_read but no explicit service policy, relying on Supabase’s service-role bypass semantics; this hurts portability and policy clarity.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md notes 'A few tables have RLS enabled with anon_read but no service_* policy.'
findings.recommendation: Add a uniform service policy: CREATE POLICY service_full_access ON pds.<table> FOR ALL TO service_role USING (true) WITH CHECK (true); keep anon_read SELECT as-is.

findings.title: hrv_predictions PK blocks multi-run history expected by views
findings.description: Production lists PK(prediction_date, model, horizon_days), but views/docs assume multiple rows per triple with DISTINCT ON picking the freshest; current PK forces overwrite or write failures.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: 'pds.hrv_predictions PK (prediction_date, model, horizon_days)'; sql/hrv_predictions_latest.sql comment: 'keeps every row from every run... DISTINCT ON picks the latest.'
findings.recommendation: Migrate to a surrogate PK and make versioned uniqueness explicit: ALTER TABLE pds.hrv_predictions ADD COLUMN id BIGSERIAL; ALTER TABLE pds.hrv_predictions DROP CONSTRAINT IF EXISTS hrv_predictions_pkey; ALTER TABLE pds.hrv_predictions ADD PRIMARY KEY (id); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX uq_hrv_pred_triple_version ON pds.hrv_predictions(prediction_date, model, horizon_days, COALESCE(model_version,'')); Update ETL to not upsert on the triple.

findings.title: Missing covering index for hrv_predictions_latest tiebreak
findings.description: The DISTINCT ON ORDER BY uses a CASE on model_version; without an expression index Postgres may sort a large working set as rows grow.
findings.evidence: ORDER BY prediction_date, model, horizon_days, CASE WHEN model_version LIKE 'backtest%' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END, created_at DESC.
findings.recommendation: Add an expression index to match the sort: CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_hrv_pred_latest ON pds.hrv_predictions (prediction_date, model, horizon_days, (model_version LIKE 'backtest%'), created_at DESC);

findings.title: Duplicate/low-value indexes that mirror PKs
findings.description: Several single-column indexes duplicate leading PK columns with DESC sort only, adding write overhead without improving common queries.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: myfitnesspal_nutrition PK(calendar_date) + idx(calendar_date); weight_log PK(log_date) + idx(log_date DESC); whoop_journal PK(cycle_date, question) + idx(cycle_date).
findings.recommendation: Drop duplicates: DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_mfp_nutrition_date; DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_weight_log_date; DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_whoop_journal_date; retain more selective/behavioral-date indexes.

findings.title: Behavioral matrix repeats LATERAL+LIMIT dedup blocks
findings.description: The view repeats near-identical LATERAL de-dupe snippets (best sleep, most-recent HRV, longest cycle), which is correct but raises maintenance cost and drift risk.
findings.evidence: Five LATERAL subqueries in daily_health_matrix_behavioral selecting per-day representatives.
findings.recommendation: Extract reusable subviews (e.g., garmin_best_sleep_per_day, whoop_longest_cycle_per_day, garmin_hrv_per_day_latest) and join those in both matrix views to DRY and ease future changes.

findings.title: Two parallel matrix views; deprecate legacy after snapshot
findings.description: Keeping both views invites accidental use of the legacy clock-day view by new queries and doubles maintenance burden.
findings.evidence: CONTEXT.md and SCHEMA_DDL.md note both are retained; snapshot exists in pds_legacy.daily_health_matrix_v0.
findings.recommendation: Mark pds.daily_health_matrix deprecated and plan removal: migrate remaining consumers, keep pds_legacy.daily_health_matrix_v0 as rollback, then DROP the legacy view and references.

findings.title: Trigger sprawl for ADR-0001 onyx_* population
findings.description: 11+ nearly-identical set_onyx_dates_* trigger functions are easy to drift over time and harder to test as a unit.
findings.evidence: SCHEMA_DDL.md: 'set_onyx_dates_* BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE triggers (11 of these), each calling derive_onyx_dates.'
findings.recommendation: Consolidate into 2–3 generic trigger functions by data shape: (instant+offset), (instant-only), and (date-only), each parameterized where feasible; replace per-table clones to reduce drift.

findings.title: Constrain enumerations (meal_events.kind, etc.)
findings.description: Free-text enums invite typos that later pivot logic won’t recognize (e.g., 'Last_meal' vs 'last_meal').
findings.evidence: meal_schema.sql: kind TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'last_meal' with no CHECK.
findings.recommendation: Add CHECKs: ALTER TABLE pds.meal_events ADD CONSTRAINT meal_kind_chk CHECK (kind IN ('last_meal','first_meal','snack','other')); consider similar checks for small-domain fields elsewhere (score_state, mood).

findings.title: WHOOP journal behaviors_date cycle pick can be ambiguous
findings.description: compute_journal_behaviors_date() picks the first cycle by start_time for a given cycle_date; on transition days two cycles may map, and the shortest may be chosen.
findings.evidence: whoop_schema.sql: ORDER BY c.start_time LIMIT 1 in compute_journal_behaviors_date().
findings.recommendation: Mirror the matrix logic and prefer the longest cycle: change ORDER BY (c.end_time - c.start_time) DESC NULLS LAST, c.start_time DESC LIMIT 1; then re-run the backfill trigger to refresh historical rows.

things_done_well.title: Centralized behavioral-day derivation with provenance
things_done_well.why_it_matters: derive_onyx_dates() and tz_for_instant() encode complex TZ/behavioral attribution once, reducing app code complexity and making attribution auditable and testable.

things_done_well.title: Use of LATERAL + LIMIT for per-day representative picks
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Choosing 'best sleep' or 'longest cycle' at query-time prevents duplicates from polluting features without pre-aggregation loss of detail.

things_done_well.title: Sentinel/diagnostic views to catch upstream drift
things_done_well.why_it_matters: tz_log_gaps and hrv_prediction_gaps make silent sources of analytical error visible quickly, aligning with the 'catch at write-time or early' philosophy.

things_done_well.title: JSONB used where schemas are open-ended, with GIN
things_done_well.why_it_matters: Keeping deeply nested supplement ingredients in JSONB with GIN enables flexible queries without forcing brittle column explosion; promotion paths exist via views.

things_done_well.title: Vector search configured idiomatically
things_done_well.why_it_matters: HNSW on embedding(1024) with cosine ops is a correct, performant choice for semantic search over personal journal entries.

questions_for_followup: Is pds.garmin_activities.activity_id globally unique in practice? If yes, we can safely add UNIQUE(activity_id) to support the laps→activities FK.
Should pds.hrv_predictions retain full run history per (date, model, horizon)? If yes, we’ll migrate to a surrogate PK; if no, we can keep the composite PK and simplify the 'latest' views.
Does the Spotify ETL guarantee dims (tracks/artists) are upserted before plays in the same job? If not, should we mark the new FKs DEFERRABLE INITIALLY DEFERRED and/or load dims first?
Are there known orphan rows today (e.g., whoop_sleep without matching cycles) that would fail FK VALIDATE? If so, should we soft-delete or fix them before enabling constraints?
Any consumer still requires the legacy pds.daily_health_matrix? If not, can we schedule its deprecation using the existing pds_legacy snapshot as safety net?
